English Presentation — Campus Security

The Privacy
vs. Safety
Paradox

A deep dive into biometric surveillance, student anonymity, and the systems we build to feel safe.

The Problem

The Challenge

Following a security threat on campus, the university wants to install biometric facial tracking on all students at all times. This creates a direct conflict between two essential rights.

Safety

The administration argues that constant surveillance ensures physical campus safety, enabling rapid identification of threats and preventing dangerous incidents from escalating.

Privacy

Students hold the fundamental right to move freely without being tracked. Mass biometric collection creates a chilling effect, psychological harm, and enables serious misuse of personal identity data.

Visual Evidence

The Reality of Surveillance

Being Watched
Identity vs. Anonymity
Data in Motion

Our Goal

The Project

Design a compromise system that guarantees physical campus safety while absolutely protecting student anonymity. The system must prove that security and privacy are not mutually exclusive — with the right architecture, you can have both.

Our Approach

The Facets

Evidence

The Chilling Effect

Research into the psychological impact of constant surveillance — how being watched suppresses free expression, alters behavior, and creates lasting mental health damage on students.

Argument

The Case Against

Persuading the administration that surrendering fundamental privacy does not actually guarantee safety — and that the harm from mass surveillance outweighs any security benefit.

Design

The Schematic

A technical diagram showing how anonymized heat-mapping works — detecting threats and crowd anomalies without ever storing personal identity data.

The Creative Twist

The X-Factor

The system must be entirely opt-in. Rather than forcing participation, we design a campus culture so rewarding and transparent that students genuinely want to be part of the safety network.

  • No mandatory biometric enrollment for any student
  • Anonymized heat-mapping as the default detection mode
  • Reward-based incentives for voluntary participation
  • Full data transparency — students see exactly what is collected
  • Safety reframed as community responsibility, not surveillance
System Architecture
Participation modeOpt-In Only
Identity storageAnonymized
Threat detectionHeat Mapping
Incentive systemGamified
Data retentionNone — Real-time only
